summary: I fled Laos because of the suffering under the Communist Party of Laos' rule. Before they took over, I served as General Commissioner for Rural Development and later as a senator. The communist occupation made life unbearable, and although I worked briefly for their government, I was always looking for a chance to leave. It was dangerous to try to escape, but eventually, I found an opportunity and became a refugee.

entity: Royal Lao Institute of Law and Administration
summary: While I was working in Laos, I taught at the Royal Lao Institute of Law and Administration. This was during the time before the communist takeover, when I was also involved in rural development. Teaching there allowed me to contribute to the education of future leaders in my country. It was an important part of my career before the political situation forced me to leave.
cues: Royal Lao Institute of Law and Administration

summary: I served as a senator in the Senate of Laos before the communist regime took control. This role came after my work in rural development and teaching. Being a senator gave me a chance to influence national policies, but the political changes made it impossible to continue. Eventually, the communist takeover forced me to seek refuge abroad.

I graduated from the University of Paris, which was a significant milestone in my education. Studying there gave me a broader perspective and prepared me to serve my country in various capacities. After returning to Laos, I was determined to improve the living conditions of my people, who were struggling with poverty. My education was a foundation for my work in government and development.

I worked for the United Nations Development Programme as a program officer for five years before fleeing Laos. I asked them to recruit me because I was waiting for a safe opportunity to leave the communist regime. This job gave me an international connection and a way to eventually escape to the United States. It was a crucial step in my journey to becoming a refugee.

My sister's family lived in St. Paul, Minnesota, and I asked to visit them as a way to leave Laos. Initially, I did not intend to become a refugee, but once I arrived in the United States, I realized it was a good opportunity to stay away from the communist regime. St. Paul became a place of refuge and a connection to my family abroad. It helped me start a new life in America.

When I came to the United States, I was thinking about escaping the communist regime in Laos. The U.S. offered me a chance to live freely and safely, so I decided to stay and become a refugee. Since arriving, I have been proud to see Laotians in America recognize their origins and strengthen ties between our two countries. The United States became my new home and a place of hope.

Laos was my homeland, a very poor country where I worked hard to improve the lives of rural communities. I served as General Commissioner for Rural Development, taught at the Royal Lao Institute of Law and Administration, and was a senator before the communist takeover. The suffering under communist rule forced me to flee, but I remain proud of my origins and hopeful for Laos' future. My experiences there shaped my commitment to development and justice.
